title: Max of minimums
description: Find max of minimums for all window sizes

examples.explanation: Array: [3,1,2,4], n=4. Window size 1: Windows: [3] min=3, [1] min=1, [2] min=2, [4] min=4. Max of mins = 4. Window size 2: Windows: [3,1] min=1, [1,2] min=1, [2,4] min=2. Max of mins = 2. Window size 3: Windows: [3,1,2] min=1, [1,2,4] min=1. Max of mins = 1. Window size 4: Windows: [3,1,2,4] min=1. Max of mins = 1. Result: [4,2,1,1].

Array: [5,3,2,6,1], n=5. Window size 1 has minimums [5,3,2,6,1], so the maximum of these minimums is 6. Window size 2 has minimums [3,2,2,1], so the maximum is 3. Window size 3 has minimums [2,2,1], so the maximum is 2. Window size 4 has minimums [2,1], so the maximum is 2. Window size 5 has minimum 1. Result: [6,3,2,2,1].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n³)
approaches.summary: For each window size from 1 to n, slide a window of that size across the array, compute the minimum in each window, then track the maximum of those minimums. This is the most straightforward but inefficient method.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force O(n³) Solution for Max of Minimums
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the brute force approach to find the maximum of minimums for all window sizes.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the problem by systematically examining all possible windows of every size in the array. For each window size from 1 to n (where n is the array length), we slide a window of that size across the array. For each window position, we compute the minimum element within that window. Then, for each window size, we track the maximum value among all the minimums found for that specific window size. Finally, we return an array where each element corresponds to the maximum of minimums for that window size. The intuition is straightforward: we're exhaustively checking every possible window configuration to guarantee we find the correct answer. This approach works because it considers all possibilities without any optimization, making it conceptually simple but computationally expensive. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty result array. 2. For each window size k from 1 to n: a. Initialize max_min = -∞. b. For each starting index i from 0 to n-k: i. Initialize window_min = ∞. ii. For each element j from i to i+k-1: window_min = min(window_min, arr[j]). iii. max_min = max(max_min, window_min). c. Append max_min to result. 3. Return result. Time complexity is O(n³) because we have three nested loops: window sizes (n), starting positions (n-k ≈ n), and window elements (k ≈ n). Space complexity is O(1) excluding the output array, as we only use a few variables. This connects to the parent problem as the complete solution using the specified approach. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, incorrect initialization of min/max values, and misunderstanding that we need results for all window sizes. Real code pattern: def max_of_minimums(arr): n = len(arr); result = []; for k in range(1, n+1): max_min = float('-inf'); for i in range(n-k+1): window_min = float('inf'); for j in range(i, i+k): window_min = min(window_min, arr[j]); max_min = max(max_min, window_min); result.append(max_min); return result.

examples.trace: Array: [3,1,2,4], n=4. Window size 1: Windows: [3] min=3, [1] min=1, [2] min=2, [4] min=4. Max of mins = 4. Window size 2: Windows: [3,1] min=1, [1,2] min=1, [2,4] min=2. Max of mins = 2. Window size 3: Windows: [3,1,2] min=1, [1,2,4] min=1. Max of mins = 1. Window size 4: Windows: [3,1,2,4] min=1. Max of mins = 1. Result: [4,2,1,1].

Array: [5,3,2,6,1], n=5. Window size 1: windows [5],[3],[2],[6],[1], minimums 5,3,2,6,1, max 6. Window size 2: windows [5,3],[3,2],[2,6],[6,1], minimums 3,2,2,1, max 3. Window size 3: windows [5,3,2],[3,2,6],[2,6,1], minimums 2,2,1, max 2. Window size 4: windows [5,3,2,6],[3,2,6,1], minimums 2,1, max 2. Window size 5: window [5,3,2,6,1], minimum 1. Result: [6,3,2,2,1].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Computing Minimum for a Given Window
ladders.desc: Find the minimum element in a contiguous subarray (window) of specified size and starting index.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach, as it performs the minimum computation for each window. Given an array, a starting index, and a window size, we need to iterate through all elements in that window to find the smallest value. The intuition is simple: by examining each element in the window and keeping track of the smallest seen so far, we can guarantee finding the minimum. This works because we're performing a linear scan over the window elements, which is a fundamental operation in array processing. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize min_val to a very large number (e.g., infinity). 2. For each index j from start to start+size-1 (inclusive): a. If array[j] < min_val, set min_val = array[j]. 3. Return min_val. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the window size, as we iterate through k elements. Space complexity is O(1), using only a few variables. This connects to the level above (L0) as it's called repeatedly for each window in the full solution. Common mistakes include incorrect loop boundaries (off-by-one errors), not initializing min_val properly (e.g., to 0 which might be smaller than actual values), and assuming the window fits within array bounds (which it should if called correctly). Real code pattern: def window_min(arr, start, size): min_val = float('inf'); for j in range(start, start+size): min_val = min(min_val, arr[j]); return min_val.

examples.trace: Array: [3,1,2,4], start=0, size=2. Window elements: indices 0 and 1: values 3 and 1. Initialize min_val = inf. j=0: arr[0]=3, min_val=min(inf,3)=3. j=1: arr[1]=1, min_val=min(3,1)=1. Return 1.

Array: [5,3,2,6,1], start=2, size=3. Window elements: indices 2,3,4: values 2,6,1. Initialize min_val = inf. j=2: arr[2]=2, min_val=min(inf,2)=2. j=3: arr[3]=6, min_val=min(2,6)=2. j=4: arr[4]=1, min_val=min(2,1)=1. Return 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sliding Window for All Sizes
ladders.desc: Iterate through all window sizes and starting positions to cover every possible window.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the sliding window mechanism that generates all possible windows in the array. The core logic involves two nested loops: one for window size and one for starting index. For each window size k from 1 to n, we consider all starting indices i from 0 to n-k, which ensures the window fits within the array bounds. This generates every contiguous subarray of the array. The intuition is that to solve the problem, we need to examine all windows, and this double loop systematically enumerates them without repetition or omission. It works because the outer loop controls the window length, and the inner loop slides the window of that length across the array. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. For k from 1 to n: a. For i from 0 to n-k: i. Window covers indices i to i+k-1. ii. Perform some operation on this window (e.g., compute minimum). Time complexity is O(n²) for generating all windows, as there are approximately n²/2 windows. Space complexity is O(1) for the loops. This connects to L1 as it provides the windows for which we compute minima, and to L0 as it's part of the full solution. Common mistakes include incorrect loop limits (e.g., using i < n instead of i <= n-k), misunderstanding that window size k can be up to n, and not handling edge cases like empty arrays. Real code pattern: n = len(arr); for k in range(1, n+1): for i in range(n-k+1): window = arr[i:i+k]; # or process directly.

examples.trace: Array: [3,1,2,4], n=4. Window size k=1: i from 0 to 3: i=0: window indices 0-0: [3]; i=1: [1]; i=2: [2]; i=3: [4]. Window size k=2: i from 0 to 2: i=0: indices 0-1: [3,1]; i=1: [1,2]; i=2: [2,4]. Window size k=3: i from 0 to 1: i=0: indices 0-2: [3,1,2]; i=1: indices 1-3: [1,2,4]. Window size k=4: i from 0 to 0: i=0: indices 0-3: [3,1,2,4].
Array: [5,3,2], n=3. Window size k=1: i from 0 to 2: i=0: [5]; i=1: [3]; i=2: [2]. Window size k=2: i from 0 to 1: i=0: [5,3]; i=1: [3,2]. Window size k=3: i from 0 to 0: i=0: [5,3,2].

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Maximum in a Collection
ladders.desc: Compute the maximum value from a set of numbers, such as the minimums found for a specific window size.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves tracking the maximum value among a series of computed minima for a given window size. After computing the minimum for each window of size k, we need to find the largest of these minimum values. The intuition is straightforward: we iterate through all the minimums and keep updating a variable with the highest value seen so far. This works because by comparing each new minimum to the current maximum, we ensure that by the end, we have the overall maximum. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize max_val to a very small number (e.g., -infinity). 2. For each minimum value min_val in the collection: a. If min_val > max_val, set max_val = min_val. 3. Return max_val. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the number of minimums (which is n-k+1 for window size k). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as we apply this to the minima computed for each window size, and to L0 as it's used to get the final result for each window size. Common mistakes include initializing max_val to 0 (which might be larger than all negative minimums), not updating max_val correctly, or confusing maximum with minimum. Real code pattern: max_val = float('-inf'); for min_val in minima_list: max_val = max(max_val, min_val); return max_val.

examples.trace: List: [3,1,2,4]. Initialize max_val = -inf. First min_val=3: max_val = max(-inf,3)=3. Second min_val=1: max_val = max(3,1)=3. Third min_val=2: max_val = max(3,2)=3. Fourth min_val=4: max_val = max(3,4)=4. Return 4.
List: [2,2,1]. Initialize max_val = -inf. First min_val=2: max_val = max(-inf,2)=2. Second min_val=2: max_val = max(2,2)=2. Third min_val=1: max_val = max(2,1)=2. Return 2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Finding Minimum in a Small Set
ladders.desc: Determine the smallest value among two or more numbers, such as within a window.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental to computing the minimum in a window. Given a set of numbers, we need to find the smallest one by comparing each number to a running minimum. The intuition is simple: start with an initial large value and update it whenever we encounter a smaller number. This works because by checking each element, we guarantee that no smaller value is missed. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize min_val to a large number (e.g., infinity). 2. For each number num in the set: a. If num < min_val, set min_val = num. 3. Return min_val. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the size of the set. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as it's used to compute each window's minimum, and to L1 as the core of the window_min function. Common mistakes include initializing min_val to 0 (which might be smaller than all positive numbers), not updating min_val correctly, or using the wrong comparison operator. Real code pattern: min_val = float('inf'); for num in numbers: if num < min_val: min_val = num; return min_val.

examples.trace: Numbers: [3,1,2]. Initialize min_val = inf. First num=3: 3 < inf, so min_val=3. Second num=1: 1 < 3, so min_val=1. Third num=2: 2 < 1? No, so min_val stays 1. Return 1.
Numbers: [5,3,2,6]. Initialize min_val = inf. First num=5: min_val=5. Second num=3: 3<5, min_val=3. Third num=2: 2<3, min_val=2. Fourth num=6: 6<2? No, min_val stays 2. Return 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Iterating Through an Array
ladders.desc: Basic loop to access each element in an array sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: using a loop to traverse an array from start to end. The intuition is that arrays are ordered collections, and we often need to process each element one by one. By using an index variable that increments from 0 to n-1 (where n is the array length), we can access each element in order. This works because arrays provide constant-time access to elements via indices. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Get the length n of the array. 2. For i from 0 to n-1: a. Access array[i] and perform some operation (e.g., print it). Time complexity is O(n) for traversing n elements. Space complexity is O(1) for the loop variable. This connects to L4 as iteration is used to find minima, and to all higher levels as it's fundamental to array processing. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using i <= n instead of i < n), confusing zero-based indexing, or not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: n = len(arr); for i in range(n): element = arr[i]; # process element.

examples.trace: Array: [3,1,2], n=3. i=0: access arr[0]=3. i=1: access arr[1]=1. i=2: access arr[2]=2. End of loop.
Array: [5], n=1. i=0: access arr[0]=5. End of loop.

approaches.name: Monotonic Stack O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a monotonic stack to find the next smaller element on left and right for each element. For each element, determine the maximum window size where it is the minimum, then update the answer for that window size. Finally, compute the maximum of minimums across all window sizes.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Max of Minimums Using Monotonic Stack
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find maximum of minimums for all window sizes using monotonic stack approach.
ladders.explanation:
The problem requires finding, for each window size k (1 ≤ k ≤ n), the maximum value among all minimum elements in all windows of size k, then returning these maximums. The naive O(n³) approach is inefficient. The monotonic stack approach leverages the observation that each element arr[i] is the minimum for certain windows. For each element, we find the maximum window size where it's the minimum by determining how far it can extend left and right before encountering a smaller element. This is done using two monotonic stacks: one to find the next smaller element on the left (left[i]) and one for the right (right[i]). The window size where arr[i] is minimum is right[i] - left[i] - 1. We then update ans[windowSize] = max(ans[windowSize], arr[i]). Finally, we propagate larger values to smaller window sizes and return the result.

Intuition: Each element dominates as the minimum in windows where no smaller element is present within its boundaries. The stacks efficiently find these boundaries in O(n) time.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize arrays left and right with -1 and n respectively.
2. Use a monotonic increasing stack to find left boundaries: for each i, pop while stack not empty and arr[stack.top] >= arr[i], then set left[i] = stack.top if stack not empty else -1, push i.
3. Similarly, find right boundaries by iterating from right to left.
4. For each i, compute windowSize = right[i] - left[i] - 1, update ans[windowSize] = max(ans[windowSize], arr[i]).
5. Propagate: for i from n-1 down to 1, ans[i] = max(ans[i], ans[i+1]).
6. Return ans[1..n].

Time: O(n) for two passes and propagation. Space: O(n) for stacks and arrays.

Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of finding next smaller elements.

Common mistakes: Not handling equal elements correctly (use >= for left, > for right to avoid double-counting), forgetting propagation step, off-by-one errors in window size calculation.

Real code pattern:
def maxOfMins(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    left = [-1]*n
    right = [n]*n
    stack = []
    for i in range(n):
        while stack and arr[stack[-1]] >= arr[i]:
            stack.pop()
        left[i] = stack[-1] if stack else -1
        stack.append(i)
    stack.clear()
    for i in range(n-1, -1, -1):
        while stack and arr[stack[-1]] >= arr[i]:
            stack.pop()
        right[i] = stack[-1] if stack else n
        stack.append(i)
    ans = [0]*(n+1)
    for i in range(n):
        window = right[i] - left[i] - 1
        ans[window] = max(ans[window], arr[i])
    for i in range(n-1, 0, -1):
        ans[i] = max(ans[i], ans[i+1])
    return ans[1:]

examples.trace:
Step 1: Find left boundaries: arr=[3,1,2,4], left=[-1,-1,1,2]
- i=0: stack=[], left[0]=-1, push 0
- i=1: pop 0 (3>=1), stack=[], left[1]=-1, push 1
- i=2: stack top 1 (1<2), left[2]=1, push 2
- i=3: stack top 2 (2<4), left[3]=2, push 3
Step 2: Find right boundaries: right=[1,4,3,4]
- i=3: stack=[], right[3]=4, push 3
- i=2: pop 3 (4>=2), stack=[], right[2]=4, push 2
- i=1: pop 2 (2>=1), stack=[], right[1]=4, push 1
- i=0: stack top 1 (1<3), right[0]=1, push 0
Step 3: Compute windows and update ans (size 5 initialized to 0):
- i=0: window=1-(-1)-1=1, ans[1]=max(0,3)=3
- i=1: window=4-(-1)-1=4, ans[4]=max(0,1)=1
- i=2: window=4-1-1=2, ans[2]=max(0,2)=2
- i=3: window=4-2-1=1, ans[1]=max(3,4)=4
Step 4: Propagate: ans=[0,4,2,1,1], for i=3 to 1: ans[3]=max(1,1)=1, ans[2]=max(2,1)=2, ans[1]=max(4,2)=4
Step 5: Return ans[1:]=[4,2,1,1]

Step 1: left boundaries: arr=[1,2,3,4], left=[-1,0,1,2]
- i=0: left[0]=-1, push 0
- i=1: stack top 0 (1<2), left[1]=0, push 1
- i=2: stack top 1 (2<3), left[2]=1, push 2
- i=3: stack top 2 (3<4), left[3]=2, push 3
Step 2: right boundaries: right=[4,4,4,4]
- i=3: right[3]=4, push 3
- i=2: stack top 3 (4>=3), pop, stack=[], right[2]=4, push 2
- i=1: stack top 2 (3>=2), pop, stack=[], right[1]=4, push 1
- i=0: stack top 1 (2>=1), pop, stack=[], right[0]=4, push 0
Step 3: Update ans:
- i=0: window=4-(-1)-1=4, ans[4]=max(0,1)=1
- i=1: window=4-0-1=3, ans[3]=max(0,2)=2
- i=2: window=4-1-1=2, ans[2]=max(0,3)=3
- i=3: window=4-2-1=1, ans[1]=max(0,4)=4
Step 4: Propagate: ans=[0,4,3,2,1], for i=3 to 1: ans[3]=max(2,1)=2, ans[2]=max(3,2)=3, ans[1]=max(4,3)=4
Step 5: Return [4,3,2,1]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding Next Smaller Elements with Monotonic Stack
ladders.desc: Find for each element the indices of next smaller element on left and right.
ladders.explanation:
This subroutine is the core of the full solution. For each element in an array, we need to find the nearest index to the left where the value is smaller (or equal, depending on implementation) and the nearest index to the right where the value is smaller. This defines the range where the current element can be the minimum. We use a monotonic increasing stack (where elements are in increasing order from bottom to top) to achieve this in O(n) time.

Intuition: As we traverse the array, the stack maintains indices of elements that haven't yet found their next smaller element to the right (when traversing left-to-right for left boundaries, or right-to-left for right boundaries). When we encounter a smaller element, it becomes the next smaller for elements in the stack that are larger, allowing us to pop them and record boundaries.

Algorithm for left boundaries (next smaller on left):
1. Initialize left array with -1.
2. Initialize empty stack.
3. For i from 0 to n-1:
   a. While stack not empty and arr[stack.top] >= arr[i], pop from stack.
   b. If stack empty, left[i] = -1, else left[i] = stack.top.
   c. Push i onto stack.
For right boundaries, traverse from n-1 to 0 similarly, initializing right with n.

Time: O(n) as each element pushed and popped at most once. Space: O(n) for stack and output arrays.

Connection to L0: These boundaries are used to compute window sizes where each element is minimum.

Common mistakes: Using wrong comparison (e.g., > instead of >= for left), not handling empty stack correctly, confusion between indices and values.

Real code pattern:
def nextSmallerLeft(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    left = [-1]*n
    stack = []
    for i in range(n):
        while stack and arr[stack[-1]] >= arr[i]:
            stack.pop()
        left[i] = stack[-1] if stack else -1
        stack.append(i)
    return left

examples.trace:
arr=[3,1,2,4]
Step 1: i=0, stack=[], left[0]=-1, push 0 → stack=[0]
Step 2: i=1, arr[stack[-1]=0]=3 >= arr[1]=1 → pop 0, stack=[], left[1]=-1, push 1 → stack=[1]
Step 3: i=2, arr[1]=1 < arr[2]=2 → left[2]=1, push 2 → stack=[1,2]
Step 4: i=3, arr[2]=2 < arr[3]=4 → left[3]=2, push 3 → stack=[1,2,3]
Result: left=[-1,-1,1,2]

arr=[3,1,2,4], traverse right-to-left
Step 1: i=3, stack=[], right[3]=4, push 3 → stack=[3]
Step 2: i=2, arr[stack[-1]=3]=4 >= arr[2]=2 → pop 3, stack=[], right[2]=4, push 2 → stack=[2]
Step 3: i=1, arr[2]=2 >= arr[1]=1 → pop 2, stack=[], right[1]=4, push 1 → stack=[1]
Step 4: i=0, arr[1]=1 < arr[0]=3 → right[0]=1, push 0 → stack=[1,0]
Result: right=[1,4,3,4]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Determining Window Size Where Element is Minimum
ladders.desc: Given left and right boundaries, compute the maximum window size where an element is the minimum.
ladders.explanation:
Once we have the left and right boundaries for each element (from L1), we can determine the range where this element is the minimum. The left boundary is the index of the nearest smaller element to the left (or -1 if none), and the right boundary is the index of the nearest smaller element to the right (or n if none). The element arr[i] is the minimum in any window that lies completely within (left[i], right[i]), i.e., windows that start after left[i] and end before right[i]. The maximum such window size is the length of this interval: windowSize = right[i] - left[i] - 1.

Intuition: No element smaller than arr[i] exists within (left[i], right[i]), so arr[i] is the minimum in any subarray within this range. The boundaries act as barriers where a smaller element appears, limiting how far the window can extend.

Algorithm:
1. Input: arr, left array, right array.
2. Initialize ans array of size n+1 with 0.
3. For each i from 0 to n-1:
   a. windowSize = right[i] - left[i] - 1
   b. ans[windowSize] = max(ans[windowSize], arr[i])
4. Output: ans array where ans[k] holds the maximum of minimums for window size k (to be propagated later).

Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

Connection to L1: Uses boundaries computed by next smaller subroutine.

Common mistakes: Off-by-one in window size calculation, not updating ans with max correctly.

Real code pattern:
def computeWindows(arr, left, right):
    n = len(arr)
    ans = [0]*(n+1)
    for i in range(n):
        window = right[i] - left[i] - 1
        ans[window] = max(ans[window], arr[i])
    return ans

examples.trace:
n=4, ans=[0,0,0,0,0]
Step 1: i=0, window=1-(-1)-1=1, ans[1]=max(0,3)=3
Step 2: i=1, window=4-(-1)-1=4, ans[4]=max(0,1)=1
Step 3: i=2, window=3-1-1=1, ans[1]=max(3,2)=3
Step 4: i=3, window=4-2-1=1, ans[1]=max(3,4)=4
Final ans before propagation: [0,4,0,0,1].

ladders.title: Building Block — Monotonic Stack Fundamentals
ladders.desc: Implement a monotonic increasing stack to process elements in linear time.
ladders.explanation:
A monotonic stack is a stack where elements are maintained in increasing or decreasing order. For this problem, we use an increasing stack (from bottom to top). It helps find the next smaller element efficiently by keeping only relevant indices. As we traverse the array, we compare the current element with the top of the stack. If the current element is smaller, it becomes the next smaller for the top element, so we pop until the stack is empty or the top is smaller. This ensures each element is pushed and popped at most once, giving O(n) time.

Intuition: The stack stores indices of elements that are waiting to find their next smaller element. When a smaller element arrives, it resolves the waiting for larger elements, allowing us to record boundaries.

Algorithm for increasing stack (left-to-right):
1. Initialize empty stack.
2. For each index i in array:
   a. While stack not empty and arr[stack.top] >= arr[i], pop from stack and process (e.g., record right boundary for popped element if traversing right-to-left).
   b. Push i onto stack.
This can be adapted for left or right boundaries by traversal direction and comparison.

Time: O(n). Space: O(n) worst-case.

Connection to L2: This stack is used to compute boundaries.

Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle equal elements (use >= for non-strict), incorrect traversal order, not clearing stack between passes.

Real code pattern:
def monotonicIncreasingStack(arr):
    stack = []
    for i in range(len(arr)):
        while stack and arr[stack[-1]] >= arr[i]:
            top = stack.pop()
            # Process top, e.g., record boundary
        stack.append(i)
    return stack

examples.trace:
arr=[3,1,2,4]
Step 1: i=0, stack=[], push 0 → stack=[0]
Step 2: i=1, arr[0]=3 >= arr[1]=1 → pop 0, stack=[], push 1 → stack=[1]
Step 3: i=2, arr[1]=1 < arr[2]=2 → push 2 → stack=[1,2]
Step 4: i=3, arr[2]=2 < arr[3]=4 → push 3 → stack=[1,2,3]
Final stack: [1,2,3]

arr=[4,3,2,1], traverse from i=3 to 0
Step 1: i=3, stack=[], push 3 → stack=[3]
Step 2: i=2, arr[3]=1 < arr[2]=2 → push 2 → stack=[3,2]
Step 3: i=1, arr[2]=2 < arr[1]=3 → push 1 → stack=[3,2,1]
Step 4: i=0, arr[1]=3 < arr[0]=4 → push 0 → stack=[3,2,1,0]
Final stack: [3,2,1,0]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Window Size from Boundaries
ladders.desc: Given left and right indices, calculate the length of the interval where an element is minimum.
ladders.explanation:
This is a simple arithmetic operation but crucial for the algorithm. For an element at index i, with left boundary L (index of nearest smaller element to the left, or -1 if none) and right boundary R (index of nearest smaller element to the right, or n if none), the maximum window size where this element is the minimum is R - L - 1. This represents the number of elements in the open interval (L, R), i.e., from L+1 to R-1 inclusive.

Intuition: The element can be the minimum in any subarray that lies strictly between L and R, because outside this range, a smaller element exists. The length of this interval gives the largest possible window.

Algorithm:
1. Input: L, R, where L < i < R.
2. windowSize = R - L - 1
3. Output: windowSize.

Time: O(1). Space: O(1).

Connection to L3: Uses boundaries found by monotonic stack.

Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors (e.g., using R - L instead of R - L - 1), misunderstanding that L and R are exclusive boundaries.

Real code pattern:
def windowSize(L, R):
    return R - L - 1

examples.trace: windowSize = 5 - 2 - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Array Windows and Minimums
ladders.desc: Given an array, find the minimum element in a contiguous subarray (window).
ladders.explanation:
This is the foundational concept: a window is a contiguous subarray of the original array. For a window defined by indices [start, end] (inclusive), the minimum is the smallest element in that range. For example, in array [3,1,2,4], the window from index 1 to 2 is [1,2] with minimum 1. The problem involves considering all possible windows of a given size k, finding their minimums, and then taking the maximum of those minimums.

Intuition: As window size increases, the minimum tends to decrease or stay the same because larger windows include more elements, increasing the chance of a smaller minimum. This property is used in the propagation step of the full algorithm.

Algorithm for a single window:
1. Input: array arr, start index s, end index e.
2. minVal = arr[s]
3. For i from s+1 to e:
   a. If arr[i] < minVal, update minVal = arr[i]
4. Output: minVal.
For all windows of size k, iterate s from 0 to n-k and compute min for each.

Time: O(k) per window, O(n*k) for all windows of size k. Space: O(1).

Connection to L4: This basic operation is extended to compute maximum window size where an element is minimum.

Common mistakes: Confusing window size with indices, not handling empty arrays.

Real code pattern:
def windowMin(arr, s, e):
    minVal = arr[s]
    for i in range(s+1, e+1):
        if arr[i] < minVal:
            minVal = arr[i]
    return minVal

examples.trace:
Window elements: indices 0,1,2 → values [3,1,2]
Step 1: minVal = arr[0]=3
Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=1 < 3 → minVal=1
Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=2 > 1 → minVal stays 1
Output: 1

Window: indices 1,2,3 → [4,3,2]
Step 1: minVal = arr[1]=4
Step 2: i=2, arr[2]=3 < 4 → minVal=3
Step 3: i=3, arr[3]=2 < 3 → minVal=2
Output: 2

approaches.name: Deque Sliding Window O(n log n)
approaches.summary: For each window size k, use a deque to maintain the minimum in the sliding window efficiently. Compute the maximum of minimums for that k, then aggregate across all k. This approach processes each window size independently.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Max of Minimums for All Window Sizes
ladders.desc: Given an array, for each window size k from 1 to n, find the maximum of all minimum values across all windows of that size.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires computing the maximum of minimum values for every possible window size in an array. The deque sliding window O(n log n) approach solves this by processing each window size independently. For each k, we use a deque to efficiently maintain the minimum in the sliding window as it moves across the array. The deque stores indices of array elements in increasing order of their values, ensuring the front always has the minimum for the current window. We slide the window from left to right, updating the deque by removing indices that are out of the window and those whose corresponding elements are greater than the new element being added. For each window position, we record the minimum (front of deque). After processing all windows of size k, we take the maximum of these minimums. We repeat this for all k from 1 to n, storing results in an output array. The intuition is that by using a deque, we avoid recomputing minima from scratch for each window, reducing time complexity. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an array result of size n with -infinity. 2. For each k from 1 to n: a. Initialize an empty deque and an array windowMins. b. For i from 0 to n-1: i. Remove indices from front of deque if they are out of window (i - k + 1 > deque[0]). ii. Remove indices from back of deque while arr[deque[-1]] > arr[i]. iii. Append i to deque. iv. If i >= k-1, append arr[deque[0]] to windowMins. c. Set result[k-1] = max(windowMins). 3. Return result. Time complexity is O(n log n) because we process n window sizes, each taking O(n) time with the deque, but we can optimize by noting that some computations overlap, though here we treat it as independent for clarity. Space complexity is O(n) for the deque and auxiliary arrays. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of finding minima for a fixed window size repeatedly. Common mistakes include not handling deque updates correctly when elements are equal (use > not >= to keep earliest index), or forgetting to check window bounds. Real code pattern: Use collections.deque in Python for O(1) operations.

examples.trace: For k=1: windows are [1],[3],[2],[4],[5], minima are 1,3,2,4,5, max is 5. For k=2: windows [1,3],[3,2],[2,4],[4,5], minima 1,2,2,4, max is 4. For k=3: windows [1,3,2],[3,2,4],[2,4,5], minima 1,2,2, max is 2. For k=4: windows [1,3,2,4],[3,2,4,5], minima 1,2, max is 2. For k=5: window [1,3,2,4,5], min 1, max is 1. Result: [5,4,2,2,1].

For k=1: windows [3],[1],[2],[4], minima 3,1,2,4, max 4. For k=2: windows [3,1],[1,2],[2,4], minima 1,1,2, max 2. For k=3: windows [3,1,2],[1,2,4], minima 1,1, max 1. For k=4: window [3,1,2,4], min 1, max 1. Result: [4,2,1,1].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Minima for Fixed Window Size Using Deque
ladders.desc: Given an array and a fixed window size k, compute the minimum value for every sliding window of that size.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the full problem, where we efficiently find all minimums for a specific window size using a deque. The intuition is to maintain a deque that stores indices of array elements in increasing order of their values, so the front always points to the minimum of the current window. As we slide the window from left to right, we update the deque in two ways: remove indices from the front if they fall outside the window (i.e., index < current position - k + 1), and remove indices from the back if their corresponding array values are greater than the new element being added (to keep the deque sorted). This ensures O(1) amortized time per element. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty deque and an empty list for minima. 2. For each index i from 0 to n-1: a. Remove from front while deque[0] < i - k + 1. b. Remove from back while arr[deque[-1]] > arr[i]. c. Append i to deque. d. If i >= k-1 (window is full), append arr[deque[0]] to minima. 3. Return minima. Time complexity is O(n) as each index is added and removed at most once. Space complexity is O(k) for the deque. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each k to get window minima, whose maximum is then taken. Common mistakes include using >= instead of > when removing from back (which can cause issues with duplicates; using > ensures we keep the earliest index for equal values), or not checking window bounds correctly. Real code pattern: Use a while loop for removals and append for additions.

examples.trace: i=0: deque=[0], i<k-1 so no min. i=1: remove front? 0>=1-2+1=0, no. Remove back? arr[0]=3 > arr[1]=1, so pop back, deque=[]. Append 1, deque=[1]. i>=1, min=arr[1]=1. i=2: remove front? 1>=2-2+1=1, no. Remove back? arr[1]=1 > arr[2]=2, no. Append 2, deque=[1,2]. i>=1, min=arr[1]=1. i=3: remove front? 1>=3-2+1=2, yes, pop front, deque=[2]. Remove back? arr[2]=2 > arr[3]=4, no. Append 3, deque=[2,3]. i>=1, min=arr[2]=2. Minima: [1,1,2].

i=0: deque=[0], no min. i=1: remove front? 0>=1-3+1=-1, no. Remove back? arr[0]=5 > arr[1]=4, pop back, deque=[]. Append 1, deque=[1], no min. i=2: remove front? 1>=2-3+1=0, no. Remove back? arr[1]=4 > arr[2]=3, pop back, deque=[]. Append 2, deque=[2], i>=2, min=arr[2]=3. i=3: remove front? 2>=3-3+1=1, no. Remove back? arr[2]=3 > arr[3]=2, pop back, deque=[]. Append 3, deque=[3], min=arr[3]=2. i=4: remove front? 3>=4-3+1=2, no. Remove back? arr[3]=2 > arr[4]=1, pop back, deque=[]. Append 4, deque=[4], min=arr[4]=1. Minima: [3,2,1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Minimum in Sliding Window with Deque
ladders.desc: Use a deque to keep track of the minimum element in a sliding window as it moves across an array.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves using a double-ended queue (deque) to efficiently maintain the minimum value in a sliding window. The intuition is that we want to avoid recomputing the minimum from scratch for each window position, which would be O(k) per window. Instead, we store indices of array elements in the deque such that they are in increasing order of their values. The front of the deque always holds the index of the minimum element in the current window. As we slide the window, we perform two types of updates: 1. Remove indices from the front if they are no longer within the window (i.e., the index is less than the current start of the window). 2. Remove indices from the back if their corresponding array values are greater than the new element being added; this keeps the deque sorted and ensures that larger, outdated values are discarded. By doing this, each element is added and removed at most once, leading to O(n) time for processing all windows. Step-by-step for a single pass: Initialize deque, iterate through array, at each index i: check and remove out-of-window indices from front, then remove from back while current element is smaller, add current index, and if window is full, record the minimum from front. This technique is key because it reduces time complexity from O(nk) to O(n). Connection to L1: This logic is applied repeatedly for different window sizes. Common mistakes include not using a deque (e.g., using a list which has O(n) removals) or incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern: Use collections.deque for efficient pops from both ends.

examples.input: arr = [3, 1, 2], window moves from index 0 to 2, k=2
examples.trace: Start: deque empty. i=0: add index 0, deque=[0], window not full. i=1: remove front? index 0 >= 1-2+1=0, no. Remove back? arr[0]=3 > arr[1]=1, so pop back, deque=[]. Add index 1, deque=[1], window full (i>=1), min=arr[1]=1. i=2: remove front? index 1 >= 2-2+1=1, no. Remove back? arr[1]=1 > arr[2]=2, no. Add index 2, deque=[1,2], window full, min=arr[1]=1. Minima: [1,1].

i=0: deque=[0], no min. i=1: remove front? 0>=1-3+1=-1, no. Remove back? arr[0]=5 > arr[1]=3, pop back, deque=[]. Add 1, deque=[1], no min. i=2: remove front? 1>=2-3+1=0, no. Remove back? arr[1]=3 > arr[2]=4, no. Add 2, deque=[1,2], window full (i>=2), min=arr[1]=3. i=3: remove front? 1>=3-3+1=1, no. Remove back? arr[2]=4 > arr[3]=2, pop back, deque=[1]. Remove back? arr[1]=3 > arr[3]=2, pop back, deque=[]. Add 3, deque=[3], window full, min=arr[3]=2. Minima: [3,2].

ladders.title: Building Block — Sliding Window Minimum with Brute Force
ladders.desc: Compute the minimum for each sliding window of fixed size by iterating through all windows and finding min directly.
ladders.explanation: This building block introduces the concept of sliding window minima without optimization, using a brute-force approach. The intuition is to understand the basic problem: given an array and a window size k, we need the minimum value for every contiguous subarray of length k. We can do this by sliding a window across the array, and for each window position, scan all k elements to find the minimum. This is straightforward but inefficient. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty list for minima. 2. For start index from 0 to n-k: a. Initialize min_val = infinity. b. For i from start to start+k-1: update min_val = min(min_val, arr[i]). c. Append min_val to minima. 3. Return minima. Time complexity is O(n*k) because there are n-k+1 windows, each taking O(k) time. Space complexity is O(1) extra space besides the output list. This building block is important because it establishes the problem we aim to optimize with the deque. Connection to L2: The deque approach improves upon this by avoiding the O(k) scan per window. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds or not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: Use nested loops, with outer loop for start and inner loop for elements in window.

examples.trace: n=3, windows: start=0: window [2,1], min=min(2,1)=1. start=1: window [1,3], min=min(1,3)=1. Minima: [1,1].
n=4, windows: start=0: window [4,5,1], min=min(4,5,1)=1. start=1: window [5,1,2], min=min(5,1,2)=1. Minima: [1,1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find Minimum in a Subarray
ladders.desc: Given an array and a range [l, r], compute the minimum value in that contiguous subarray.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves finding the minimum element in a specified subarray of an array. The intuition is simple: iterate through the elements from index l to r inclusive, keeping track of the smallest value seen. This is a fundamental operation used in many algorithms, including sliding window problems. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize min_val = arr[l] or a large number. 2. For i from l to r: if arr[i] < min_val, update min_val = arr[i]. 3. Return min_val. Time complexity is O(r-l+1), which is linear in the size of the subarray. Space complexity is O(1). This operation is a building block for the brute-force sliding window minimum, where we repeatedly apply it to overlapping subarrays. Connection to L3: In the sliding window brute force, we call this operation for each window. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (e.g., using < instead of <=) or not handling empty ranges. Real code pattern: Use a for loop with range(l, r+1) in Python.

examples.trace: Subarray indices 0 to 2: [3,1,4]. Initialize min=3. i=0: arr[0]=3, min=3. i=1: arr[1]=1 < 3, min=1. i=2: arr[2]=4 > 1, min stays 1. Result: 1.
Subarray indices 1 to 3: [6,7,8]. Initialize min=6. i=1: arr[1]=6, min=6. i=2: arr[2]=7 > 6, min=6. i=3: arr[3]=8 > 6, min=6. Result: 6.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Minimum Element
ladders.desc: Learn how to store a list of numbers in an array and find the smallest number in it.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the basics of arrays and finding the minimum element. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements (e.g., numbers) in contiguous memory locations, accessible by indices starting from 0. To find the minimum element in an array, we iterate through all elements, comparing each to a running minimum. The intuition is simple: start with the first element as the minimum, then for each subsequent element, if it is smaller, update the minimum. This ensures we find the smallest value after one pass. Step-by-step: 1. Let arr be an array of n numbers. 2. Initialize min_val = arr[0]. 3. For i from 1 to n-1: if arr[i] < min_val, set min_val = arr[i]. 4. Return min_val. Time complexity is O(n) as we check each element once. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few variables. This is a fundamental operation in programming and is prerequisite for more complex problems like sliding windows. Connection to L4: This generalizes to finding the minimum in any subarray by adjusting the iteration range. Common mistakes include not initializing min_val properly (e.g., to 0 which might not be in the array) or using wrong comparison operators. Real code pattern: Use a for loop over the array indices.

examples.trace: Start: min_val = arr[0] = 5. i=1: arr[1]=2 < 5, min_val=2. i=2: arr[2]=8 > 2, min_val=2. i=3: arr[3]=1 < 2, min_val=1. Result: 1.
Start: min_val = arr[0] = 3. i=1: arr[1]=3 not < 3, min_val=3. i=2: arr[2]=3 not < 3, min_val=3. Result: 3.
